coredata.dc:title: Smart fluorescent tag based on amine response for non-contact and visual monitoring of seafood freshness
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yingnan
Xiao, Yaqing
Shang, Minghui
Zhuang, Yuting
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: The method of monitoring the freshness of seafood is not satisfactory for ensuring food safety. Here, we develop a filter membrane-based smart fluorescent tag with excellent biogenic amine response, which can monitor the freshness of seafood in real time and visually. We use green fluorescent fluorescein as an indicator and red fluorescent carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with aggregation-induced emission property as an internal reference. After simple mixing, they are deposited on the commercial filter membrane by physical deposition method to prepare the fluorescent tag. When amine molecules are present, the molecular conformation of fluorescein changes and the fluorescence increases rapidly, while RQDs have no obvious response, thus achieving a clear fluorescence color response to biogenic amines. Under UV light of 254 nm and 365 nm, the fluorescent tag showed sensitive, rapid, and color-responsive to ammonia in a wide range of 250–2.5 × 104 ppm. To further verify the practical application potential, the fluorescent tag was used to monitor the freshness of shrimp and crab in situ in real time, and satisfactory results were achieved. More importantly, owing to the reversible conformational change of fluorescein, the fluorescent tag can be reused and manufactured at low cost, making it a more attractive platform for freshness monitoring in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescein
Biogenic amine
Smart tag
Solid state fluorescence
Vapor-phase detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent filter membrane
Measuring ammonia using the as-prepared fluorescent filter membrane
Preparation and Characterization of the RQDs
Construction of the visual fluorescent response membrane

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of the Talented Program ( A279021724 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601 ). They would also like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS and confocal laser microscopy analysis.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of the Talented Program (A279021724) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601). They would also like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS and confocal laser microscopy analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of preparation of the R-F visual fluorescent filter membrane. (b) The amine-response behavior of the prepared R-F filter membrane.

Characterization of RQDs. (a) Photograph of RQDs powder under ambient light. (b) HR-TEM images. (c) Size distribution. (d) Raman spectrum excited by a 785 nm laser. (e) FT-IR spectra. (f) Survey XPS spectrum. (g-i) High-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1 s, S 2p, and N 1 s, respectively.

Optical characteristics of RQDs: (a-c) Photograph of the RQD ethanol aqueous solutions under ambient light, 365 nm UV light and 254 nm UV light irradiation. The volume ratio of water varies from 0% to 90%. (d, e) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of the RQD ethanol aqueous solutions with different water ratios.

(a) Photographs of blank filter membrane, RQDs modified filter membrane, fluorescein modified filter membrane, and R-F (RQDs and fluorescein) modified filter membrane under ambient light, 254 nm UV light and 365 nm UV light. (b) Schematic diagram of the morphology of RQDs on the filter membrane before and after ethanol volatilization (The circle represents the filter membrane, and the wavy line represents the membrane fiber. Blue dots represent the RQDs monomer, and the red particles represent the RQDs aggregates). (c) The SEM images of the filter membranes modified by RQDs, fluorescein and R-F. The top ones are images at a low magnification, and the bottom ones are images at a higher magnification.

(a) Photographs of RQDs, fluorescein and R-F filter membranes exposed to 25 mg/mL ammonia atmosphere for 7 min under 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps. (b) Fluorescence response of the filter membranes modified by the mixed solution of RQDs and fluorescein in different proportions to ammonia. (The ammonia concentration is 2.5 mg/mL. The concentration of RQD solution is 4 mg/mL, and the concentration of fluorescein solution is 0.5 mg/mL. Ⅰ: 4 mL of RQD solution is mixed with 0.1 mL of fluorescein solution, Ⅱ: 4 mL of RQD solution is mixed with 0.2 mL of fluorescein solution, III: 4 mL of RQD solution is mixed with 0.6 mL of fluorescein solution, Ⅳ: 4 mL of RQD solution is mixed with 1 mL of fluorescein solution, Ⅴ: 4 mL of RQD solution is mixed with 1.5 mL of fluorescein solution).

(a) The fluorescence responses of R-F filter membrane to different concentrations of ammonia under two excitation wavelengths. (b) Photographs of recycling use of the R-F membrane for 2.5 mg/mL ammonia detection under UV lamps at 254 nm and 365 nm.
Under 254 nm and 365 nm ultraviolet light, the R-F membrane serves as a smart tag to monitor the freshness of shrimp under different storage conditions.

head.ce:title: Smart fluorescent tag based on amine response for non-contact and visual monitoring of seafood freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The method of monitoring the freshness of seafood is not satisfactory for ensuring food safety. Here, we develop a filter membrane-based smart fluorescent tag with excellent biogenic amine response, which can monitor the freshness of seafood in real time and visually. We use green fluorescent fluorescein as an indicator and red fluorescent carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with aggregation-induced emission property as an internal reference. After simple mixing, they are deposited on the commercial filter membrane by physical deposition method to prepare the fluorescent tag. When amine molecules are present, the molecular conformation of fluorescein changes and the fluorescence increases rapidly, while RQDs have no obvious response, thus achieving a clear fluorescence color response to biogenic amines. Under UV light of 254 nm and 365 nm, the fluorescent tag showed sensitive, rapid, and color-responsive to ammonia in a wide range of 250–2.5 × 104 ppm. To further verify the practical application potential, the fluorescent tag was used to monitor the freshness of shrimp and crab in situ in real time, and satisfactory results were achieved. More importantly, owing to the reversible conformational change of fluorescein, the fluorescent tag can be reused and manufactured at low cost, making it a more attractive platform for freshness monitoring in the field of food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Seafood rich in protein has always been popular among consumers. As the demand for seafood in the global market continues to grow, more and more fresh seafood is being transported around the world [1]. However, inappropriate transportation and storage conditions may lead to the production of biogenic amines through microbial enzymatic processes, for instance, the decarboxylation of amino acids and the amination of carbonyl-containing organic compounds [2,3]. The accumulation of these biogenic amines can be used as a direct marker of food spoilage [4]. It is therefore critical to detect the content of biogenic amines for monitoring freshness and evaluating the quality of seafood. Over the last few decades, a variety of analytical methods for detecting biogenic amines have been developed, such as gas chromatography [5], liquid chromatography [6], electronic nose [7], electronic tongue [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], and so on. Unfortunately, most of these strategies usually rely on expensive and sophisticated instruments, cumbersome sample pretreatments, and highly trained technicians [3,10,11]. Thus, they are not suitable for real-time in-situ detection of biogenic amines, nor are they affordable solution for ordinary people. Great interest has been stimulated in developing a simple, rapid, and cost-effective method to monitor the real-time freshness of seafood in situ.

In recent years, optical detection technology, especially fluorescence technologies have shown great potentials for the assessment of food quality and safety owing to the characteristics of high sensitivity, rapid response, easy operation, low background noise and a wide working range [12–16]. Various fluorescent materials/probes have been reported that exhibited great potential for the rapid detection of biogenic amines, including gold nanoparticles [17], pyrylium salts [18], carboxylated polyfluorene [19], carbon dots [20], methyl red@lanthanide metal–organic frameworks [21], and so on. Among them, organic fluorophores have attracted the attention of scientific community, which can efficiently detect gaseous or liquid analytes. Moreover, their low cost, easy availability, fast response, high quantum yield and high efficiency enable them to be monitored in real time [22]. However, a key problem is that it is difficult to recognize a minor change in the fluorescence intensity of a single-fluorophore probe with the naked eyes, leading to a subjective decision as to whether a detection event has occurred. In addition, the detection abilities of many reported fluorescence probes are limited to the solution state, which hinders their practical application in real-time sensing. Contact testing may also cause secondary contamination of food. It is more feasible to develop a new type of probe that can effectively detect gaseous biogenic amines in a solid state.

Inspired by the above facts, in this study, we design a novel filter membrane-based fluorescent tag for visual non-contact monitoring of biogenic amines to in situ evaluate seafood freshness by naked eyes with high color resolution. As shown in Fig. 1 , green fluorescent fluorescein and red fluorescent carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect were physically deposited on the filter membrane, where fluorescein acted as an indicator and RQDs served as an internal reference. When biogenic amines molecules were present, the fluorescence of fluorescein increased rapidly due to the proton-deprotonation reaction, while RQDs did not respond to amine molecules. Thus, a simple, effective and visual fluorescent tag was constructed. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that fluorescein can be uniformly dispersed on the membrane surface and inside the pores. This not only can effectively avoid the aggregation-induced quenching caused by the accumulation of materials, but also can increase the contact possibility between fluorescein and biogenic amines molecules. The hydrophobic RQDs with AIE effect are distributed on the filter membrane in an aggregated state, which can provide good red background fluorescence for visual fluorescence analysis. In order to prove the practical application potential of this smart tag, we applied it to the freshness monitoring of shrimp and crab. The results confirm that the as-prepared visual fluorescent membrane can accurately and quickly respond to the biogenic amines released from seafood. Therefore, it readily serves as a non-contact smart tag to accurately, real-time, visually and in-situ monitor freshness in the food industry.

Dithiosalicylic acid, fluorescein, melamine, ethylenediamine, triethylamine, pyrrolidine, putrescine, morpholine, histamine, urea, benzylamine, aniline, lysine, arginine and histidine were supplied by Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd.. Ammonia (25%) and acetic acid were obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd.. Organic phase filter membrane (0.22 μm) was purchased from Shanghai Xinya Purification Device Factory.

High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA) and Nano Measurer soft-ware were used to characterize the morphology of RQDs. Fourier Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker Vetex 70, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) were applied in analyzing the element and structure. Raman spectrometer (Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution), ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Shimadzu UV-2600, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Fluoromax-4) were used to characterize the optical properties. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nano SEM-450, USA) was applied in the morphological analysis of the filter membrane. The absolute quantum yield of RQDs was measured by the Edinburgh FL S1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. Confocal laser microscopy (Leica TCS SP8) is used to characterize the distribution of fluorescent materials on the surface of the membrane.

RQDs were prepared according to a previous report with a minor change [23]. In briefly, melamine (0.302 g) and dithiosalicylic acid (0.816 g) were dispersed into acetic acid (60 mL) by sonication. Afterwards, the obtained solution was added to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and kept at 180℃ for 10 h. When the resulting solution was cooled to room temperature, it was added to 1 L of boiling water to wash the remaining raw materials and solvent. The solid RQDs was obtained by vacuum filtration and dried in vacuum at 60℃. In order to further purify RQDs, the obtained RQDs were re-dispersed in ethanol solution, and the insoluble matter was removed by centrifugation. The supernatant was vacuum-dried again to obtain purified RQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent filter membrane
ce:section.ce:para: First, an ethanol solution of RQDs (4 mg/mL) and an ethanol solution of fluorescein (0.5 mg/mL) were prepared. Then, 4 mL of RQDs solution and 1 mL of fluorescein solution were evenly mixed, and the filter membrane was carefully immersed in the solution. After 100 s, the filter membrane was taken out and dried naturally at room temperature. Furthermore, we also prepared functionalized filter membranes with different fluorescence characteristics by adjusting the concentration ratios of fluorescein and RQDs solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring ammonia using the as-prepared fluorescent filter membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia was used as a model biogenic amine to investigate the capability of the prepared fluorescent filter membrane to detect biogenic amine vapor. First, a series of ammonia solutions of known concentrations were prepared by diluting commercial ammonia with deionized water. Afterwards, the ammonia solution was added to a 24-well plate (1 mL per well), and the prepared filter membrane was immediately placed on top of the 24-well plate. The fluorescence color change was recorded with a camera. Furthermore, the reversibility and reusability of the fluorescent membrane was also evaluated by exposing it alternately to ammonia and ammonia-free environments for multiple cycles.

Fresh shrimps and crabs purchased from a local supermarket were selected as real samples for fluorescence detection of freshness. First, shrimp and crabs were placed in sterile petri dishes and sealed bags, respectively. The as-prepared fluorescent tags were then placed inside the containers. It was worth noting to avoid direct contact between the tags and the samples. After that, all containers were sealed and maintained at different temperatures (-20℃, 4℃ and 25℃) for 1 ∼ 5 days. During this period, the fluorescence changes of the smart tags under the UV lamp were recorded by a camera to analyze the freshness of the samples. In addition, all shrimp and crab meat samples were collected for the determination of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) using an automatic Kjeldahl nitrogen analyzer (Hanon Instrument, Jinan, China) [24]. All measurements were carried out in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of the RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We used melamine and dithiosalicylic acid as precursors to prepare RQDs powder through a one-pot solvothermal method combined with a simple purification process, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The morphology and structural properties of RQDs powder were characterized by HR-TEM, Raman spectroscopy, FT-IR, and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, RQDs present a uniform monodisperse dot-like distribution, with a particle size between 1.5 ∼ 3.5 nm. After counting 160 samples, it can be calculated that the average diameter of RQDs is about 2.31 nm. The HR-TEM image in the inset of Fig. 2b displays a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [25]. This data indicates that the graphite-like structure exists in RQDs, which is consistent with the report of Yang et al. [23]. The Raman spectrum of RQDs is shown in Fig. 2d. It can be seen that there are two characteristic peaks located at 1335 cm−1 (D bond) and 1576 cm−1 (G band), which are ascribed to the disordered surface and sp2 carbon network region in RQDs, respectively [25,26]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the two peaks is 2.1, confirming the amorphous surface of RQDs [27].

Furthermore, the chemical structure of the as-prepared RQDs was also analyzed by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As can be seen in Fig. 2e, there are hydrophobic functional groups such as methylene (2973 cm−1), S-H (2658 cm−1), C≡N (2067 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1682 cm−1), C = C (1462 cm−1), C-N (1416 cm−1), aromatic C-NH (1264 cm−1), C-O (1151 cm−1), C-S (694 cm−1) and S-S (494 cm−1) on the surface of RQDs [28–31]. However, the hydrophilic hydroxyl or amino functional groups (3067 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1) belonging to the two precursors of melamine and dithiosalicylic acid almost completely disappear on the surface of RQDs, indicating that RQDs are hydrophobic [32,33]. Fig. 2f displays the full XPS spectrum of RQDs. It can be seen that RQDs are composed of C (284.17 eV), O (532.17 eV), S (163.17 eV), and N (398.17 eV) elements. The corresponding atomic ratios are 69.85%, 19.58%, 9.66%, and 0.91%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2g can be deconvolved into two peaks corresponding to C–C/C = C (283.8 eV) and C = O/C = N (288.0 eV) groups, respectively [26]. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 2h is separated into three peaks located at 162.8 eV, 163.5 eV, and 164.0 eV, corresponding to S-C, S-H, and S-S groups, respectively [34]. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2i contains two peaks at 398.6 eV for pyridinic C3-N and 399.3 eV for pyrrolic C2-N–H [35,36]. The aforementioned data collectively indicate that the surface of RQDs contains almost no amino and hydroxyl groups, which is consistent with the FT-IR results.

In view of the hydrophobicity, RQDs aggregates would be formed in solutions with high water fractions and induce unique optical behaviors. We explored the optical properties of RQDs in ethanol–water solutions with different water fractions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, when the volume ratio of water in the RQD solution is less than 50%, the RQD solution is a uniform, transparent and stable pink solution under sunlight. Under 365 nm UV light, RQD solution emits bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 3b). As the water volume ratio increases, pink fluorescence gradually appears. When the volume ratio of water exceeds 50%, RQDs aggregates are formed, and the solution changes from clear to turbid. Under 365 nm UV light, the suspension emits pink fluorescence. And as the water volume ratio increases, the fluorescence gradually transitions from pink to orange-red, and then to red.

To gain insight into the unique optical properties of RQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of RQDs ethanol aqueous solutions with different water ratios were measured. As shown in Fig. 3d, the as-prepared RQDs exhibit a characteristic peak at 311 nm, which is mainly owing to the π-π* transition of C = C in the RQDs core. There is a weak peak at 542 nm, which may be related to the n-π* transition of the surface states containing C = N/C = O, C-O and C-S structures [23]. Additionally, the peak intensity at 311 nm gradually decreases with the increase of water volume ratio. It indicates that this characteristic peak is related to the dispersion of RQDs. Due to the poor dispersion in water, RQDs aggregates are formed in solutions with high water fractions. This greatly restricts the intramolecular rotation and activated the AIE process [23]. Fig. 3e displays the fluorescence spectra of the RQD solution with excitation at 365 nm. When the water volume ratio is lower than 50%, RQDs only show a fluorescence peak at 437 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence. As the water volume ratio increases, the fluorescence intensity at 437 nm continues to decrease. Upon further increasing the water fraction (more than 50%), a new fluorescence emission peak appears at 603 nm, corresponding to red fluorescence. The intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I603/I437) is positively correlated with the water volume ratio. This AIE behavior may be attributed to an enhancement of the twisted intramolecular charge transfer effect in the higher polar solution [37]. This result of the fluorescence spectra is consistent with the fluorescence photograph under a 365 nm UV lamp, which together indicates that the as-prepared RQDs possess hydrophobic property and AIE property. As the water volume ratio increases, RQDs gradually change from monomers to aggregates, showing a special fluorescence color change. The absolute quantum yield was calculated to be 4.7%.

Furthermore, the fluorescence performance of RQDs ethanol aqueous solution under 254 nm UV light was also investigated (Fig. 3c). Interestingly, the RQD solutions have no fluorescence when the volume ratio of water is less than 50%. When the water volume ratio is greater than 50%, the RQDs suspensions emit purple or pink fluorescence. Figure S1 displayed the fluorescence emission spectrum of the RQD ethanol solution with excitation at 254 nm. It can be seen that the RQD solution has no fluorescence emission when the volume ratio of water is less than 50%. When the volume ratio of water exceeds 50%, the fluorescence of the solution gradually increases as the volume ratio of water increases. This phenomenon is consistent with the photo under 254 nm UV light. This unique optical property provides the possibility to develop RQDs-based dual-modal fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the visual fluorescent response membrane
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the above researches, fluorescein with good fluorescence characteristics and RQDs with AIE effect are co-deposited on the organic phase filter to construct a visual fluorescent response membrane. Among them, fluorescein can undergo a conformational change in the presence of amine molecules to cause fluorescence enhancement, thereby acting as an indicator. The fluorescence of RQDs is not interfered by amine molecules due to the hydrophobic property. Thus, RQDs serve as an internal reference.

The prepared functionalized filter membrane was shown in Fig. 4 a. Under ambient light, the filter membranes modified by RQDs and R-F (RQDs and fluorescein) are light pink, and the fluorescein modified filter membrane is light green. These color changes reflect that the two fluorescent materials, RQDs and fluorescein, can be deposited on the filter membrane. In addition, XPS analysis (Figure S2) and laser confocal images (Figure S3) also verified the successful deposition of RQDs and fluorescein on the membrane. Next, we further investigated the fluorescence performance of the three fluorescent filter membranes. It can be seen that under UV light of 254 nm and 365 nm, the blank membrane emits blue fluorescence, while the fluorescein filter membrane exhibits green fluorescence. Both the RQDs filter membrane and the R-F filter membrane show bright pink fluorescence. It once again proves that RQDs and fluorescein are successfully deposited on the filter membrane. According to the fact that RQDs emit red fluorescence when they are fully aggregated, the appearance of pink fluorescence may be related to the co-existence of RQDs monomers and aggregates on the filter membrane. Fig. 4b is a schematic diagram of the mechanism of RQDs modified on the filter membrane, where the circle represents of the filter membrane and the wavy line represents the membrane fiber. Blue dots refer to the RQDs monomer, and the red particles represent the RQDs aggregates. When the membrane is immersed in the ethanol solution of RQDs, the monomer enters the membrane structure along the membrane fibers. With the continuous volatilization of ethanol, some of the RQDs monomers gradually aggregated and surfaced. Therefore, the RQDs modified filter membrane displays as hybrid pink fluorescence. On the other hand, the pink fluorescence may also be caused by the superposition of the blue fluorescence of the filter itself and the red fluorescence of the RQDs aggregates.

In addition, we used SEM images to characterize the successful modification of the fluorescent material on the membrane. As shown in Fig. 4c, the organic phase filter membrane presents a multilayer porous structure composed of membrane fibers, and the pores are connected to each other. After modification by RQDs, many large particles appeared on the fiber surface and in the pores, indicating that RQDs can form aggregates through the membrane fibers after ethanol volatilized. There are also some small particles on the surface of the fluorescein modified filter membrane, which may be related to the insufficient dissolution of fluorescein in ethanol. For RQDs and fluorescein co-deposited filter membrane, more particulate matter accumulated on the surface of the membrane and inside the pores, which further proves the successful modification of the fluorescent material on the membrane. The water contact angle of the prepared fluorescent filter membrane was also measured using a water contact angle goniometer (JY-PHb model, Chengde Jinhe Instrument Co., Ltd, China). As shown in Figure S4, the water contact angle of the filter membrane before modification is 42.07°±0.45°. After fluorescein modification, the water contact angle of the filter membrane did not change significantly (42.33°±0.35°). For RQDs and R-F modified filters, their water contact angles increased significantly to 50.13°±0.31° and 50.30°±2.26°. It may be related to the strong hydrophobicity of RQDs.

After successfully preparing RQDs, fluorescein and R-F functionalized filter membranes, we studied the fluorescence responses of these three membranes using ammonia as a model biogenic amine. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when the RQDs filter membrane is exposed to an ammonia atmosphere with a concentration of 25 mg/mL, the fluorescence color remained unchanged regardless of 254 nm or 365 nm UV light. It indicated that RQDs is irresponsive to ammonia and can serve as an internal reference. However, under the same conditions, the green fluorescence of the fluorescein filter membrane is significantly enhanced. This is mainly due to that a proton of fluorescein was deprived upon exposing to ammonia, which causes changes in the molecular structure [38–40]. The 1H NMR and UV–vis spectra of fluorescein (Figure S5) provide strong support for this hypothesis. Therefore, fluorescein can be used as an indicator for ammonia. For the R-F filter membrane, the fluorescence changes from pink to light green under 254 nm UV light, and the fluorescence changes from pink to grass green under 365 nm UV light. These fluorescence color changes indicated that the as-prepared R-F filter membrane can be used as a visual fluorescent tag for the visual detection of ammonia.

In order to obtain the optimum detection performance, the ratio of the two fluorescent materials and the exposure time to ammonia were optimized. As shown in Fig. 5b, no matter under the UV light of 254 nm or 365 nm, the added amount of fluorescein tested has a weak effect on the fluorescence color of the R-F filter membrane before the reaction with ammonia. When exposed to an ammonia environment, the fluorescence of the R-F filter membrane prepared with a low concentration of fluorescein does not change obviously, and it remains pink. As the concentration of fluorescein increases, green fluorescence gradually appears in the R-F filter membrane. After comprehensively considering the fluorescence color changes before and after the reaction of the R-F filter membrane at two different UV light, 4 mL of RQD solution (4 mg/mL) and 1 mL of fluorescein solution (0.5 mg/mL) mixture were selected for subsequent filter membrane preparation.

Reaction time is also an important factor affecting detection performance. We recorded the fluorescence change of the R-F membrane to ammonia within 20 min. As shown in Figure S6, under UV light at 254 nm and 365 nm, the fluorescence of the R-F membrane does not change significantly within 1 to 20 min, indicating that the prepared fluorescent membrane’s response to ammonia reaches equilibrium within 1 min. Thus, 1 min was set as the optimum response time.

Under the optimal experimental conditions discussed above, the fluorescence responses of the R-F membrane to different concentrations of ammonia at two excitation wavelengths were explored. As shown in Fig. 6 a, under a 254 nm UV lamp, the green fluorescence of fluorescein progressively dominates as the ammonia concentration increases. And a distinctive fluorescence color change from pink to light pink to gray-green to green is clearly recognized by observers without any training, indicating that the fluorescent membrane has the potential to be used for the visual detection of ammonia. The change trend of the fluorescence color of the membrane under 365 nm UV light is basically the same as that at 254 nm. The above results showed that the R-F fluorescent membrane exhibited a sensitive and fast color response to ammonia, and the color change can be observed by the naked eyes without the aid of precision instruments. The response of fluorescein membrane to ammonia under the same conditions was also measured. As shown in Figure S7, the green fluorescence intensity of the fluorescein membrane increases with the increase of the ammonia concentration. However, only the change in green fluorescence intensity is difficult to recognize and analyze with the naked eye. Hence, the as-prepared fluorescent membrane has great potential to be developed as a fluorescent tag for the monitoring of biogenic amines.

More importantly, we found that the reaction of the R-F membrane to ammonia was reversible. After the membrane was removed from the NH3 atmosphere and placed in the air for 1 min, the fluorescence of fluorescein was quenched and the color of the membrane returned to the original state (Supplementary Video 1 and Video 2). The reusability of the fluorescent membrane was further explored. As shown in Fig. 6b, the membrane has no obvious fatigue or loss of emission intensity after five cycles, demonstrating the excellent stability, ammonia reactivity, and reversibility. Thus, the R-F fluorescent membrane is suitable for practical applications in sensing systems.

The response of the fluorescent membrane for aliphatic, primary, secondary and tertiary monoamines, heterocyclic amines, and amino acids has also been investigated. As shown in Figure S8, the fluorescence intensity of R-F membrane changes significantly after exposure to NH3, ethylenediamine, and triethylamine, while other amines have no effect on the fluorescence intensity. This is mainly due to the strong volatility of NH3, ethylenediamine and triethylamine. In order to further explore the response of the fluorescent membrane, the above-mentioned substances were dropped on the surface of the membranes. It can be seen that they can cause varying degrees of changes in fluorescence intensity except for urea. These organic amines, including NH3, ethylenediamine, triethylamine, putrescine, and histamine, are important components of biogenic amines released by the degradation of amino acids [38]. Thus, the R-F membrane can act as an efficient indicator for monitoring food freshness.

After establishing excellent sensitivity to ammonia, we evaluated the practical application potential of the as-prepared visual fluorescent membrane as a smart tag for monitoring seafood freshness using shrimp and crab as examples. As shown in Fig. 7 , a piece of R-F fluorescent membrane is sealed in a plastic container containing a shrimp, and then they are kept at three different temperatures for 5 days. At 254 nm UV light, pink represents fresh, and light pink represents spoiled, and off-white represents severe spoiled. It can be seen that after storage at 25℃ for 1 day and storage at 4℃ for 3 days, the original pink fluorescence of the membrane changes to light pink, indicating that the shrimps has spoiled. However, when the shrimp is stored at −20℃ for 5 days, there is almost no obvious fluorescence color change of the membrane, indicating that the shrimp is still fresh. At an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, pink represents fresh, and yellow represents spoiled, and green represents severe spoiled. It can be seen that the R-F membrane still exhibits a sensitive fluorescence response to biogenic amines and the results are basically consistent with that at 254 nm. In order to eliminate the interference of environmental humidity on the fluorescence of the membrane, the R-F membranes are placed in the packaging boxed containing wet tissues and keep at different temperature for 5 days. As displayed in Figure S9, the fluorescence intensity of the R-F membranes remains unaltered within 5 days on matter it is under 254 nm or 365 nm UV light. Therefore, the fluorescence of the R-F membrane is hardly affected by the environmental humidity, and the above-mentioned fluorescence changes are mainly attributed to the fluorescence responses of the membrane to biogenic amines. Except the shrimp, the fluorescent membrane is also applied to the freshness monitoring of crabs. As shown in Figure S10, the fluorescent membrane shows a good detection performance for the freshness of crabs.

To verify the detection accuracy, we compared the detection result of the fluorescent membrane with that of the standard TVBN method. According to the standard, when the TVBN content is less than 120 mg/kg, raw shrimp is fresh. When the TVBN content is in the range of 120 ∼ 250 mg/kg, shrimp has been slightly decomposed but still edible. When the TVBN content exceeds 250 mg/kg, shrimp is spoiled and inedible [38]. As shown in Figure S11a, the TVBN level in the fresh shrimps is 53 mg/kg. After storage at 25℃ for 1 day, the value increases to 1040 mg/kg, indicating that shrimps have completely spoiled. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the R-F membrane changes from pink to light pink at 254 nm UV light, and from pink to yellow-green at 365 nm UV light. When the shrimps are kept at 4℃ for 3 days, the TVBN value is 350 mg/kg. The fluorescent membrane changes to light pink at 254 nm UV light and yellow at 365 nm UV light, reflecting the spoilage of the shrimps. After being kept at −20℃ for 5 days, the value increases to 86 mg/kg, but the fluorescence of the membrane hardly changes, which indicates that the shrimp is still fresh and edible. The above experimental results reveal that the fluorescent color change of the R-F membrane is consistent with the result of the standard TVBN method. We also measured the TVBN content in crabs. It can be seen that the TVBN data in Figure S11b corresponds to the fluorescence color change of the membrane, which again verifies the accuracy of the visual fluorescence detection method. Therefore, the R-F membrane can act as a smart tag to simply and accurately monitor the freshness of shrimps and crabs. Utilizing this feature, the smart tag can be easily applied in the seafood supply chain to monitor freshness in a real-time and visual manner. In the future, it is also expected to use smartphones to capture tags and evaluate freshness through more accurate color analysis.

In summary, we developed a smart fluorescent tag with excellent biogenic amine-response by depositing fluorescein and RQDs with AIE effect on the organic phase filter membrane, which can be used for rapid and visual monitoring of seafood freshness. Green-emitting fluorescein acted as a biogenic amine indicator, and red-emitting RQDs served as an internal reference. The filter membrane showed a sensitive, fast (within 1 min), and reversible color response to biogenic amines. And under different UV light, the fluorescent membrane showed different color changes. When exposed to different concentrations of biogenic amine vapor, under 254 nm UV light, the fluorescence of the membrane changed from pink to light pink, to off-white and finally to green. Under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence of the membrane changed from pink to light pink, then to yellow, and finally to green. Such a significant color change makes it possible to detect biogenic amines with the naked eye in real time and accurately. The membrane can be used as a simple, fast-responding, cost-effective, and sensitive smart tag for the visual monitor of the freshness of shrimps and crabs. The development of this material is very important for ensuring food quality safety and providing practical applications in the food industry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of the Talented Program (A279021724) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Nos. Z111021601). They would also like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS and confocal laser microscopy analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.132647.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
